prompt.task: Summarize the document for an executive audience.

constraints.constraint: Include 3 bullet points for key takeaways.
Do not add facts not present in the document.

prompt.input:
XML-Structured Prompting (XSP) is a software engineering practice for designing reliable LLM systems. The core idea is to wrap each logical section of a prompt in labeled tags, creating unambiguous structure that models can reliably parse and follow.
In enterprise contexts, XSP becomes a necessity because of auditability requirements—regulators and compliance teams need to see exactly what constraints apply to each prompt. Change control becomes meaningful when auditors can see diffs at the constraint/task/contract level, not paragraph-by-paragraph. Different teams need a common interface for prompts so they can share libraries, tools, and practices.
The VCO framework provides a mental model for XSP: Vocabulary (standardized tags), Constraints (rules and guardrails), and Output Contracts (deliverable specifications). Without vocabulary, you get muddled instructions and data. Without constraints, you get drift, fluff, and accidental policy violations. Without output contracts, you get inconsistent formatting and parsing failures.
Organizations typically adopt XSP in phases: starting with ad-hoc prompting in notebooks, moving to pattern adoption in high-value flows, then informal standardization across teams, and finally platform-wide tooling with version control, testing, and observability. The investment is a few hours to learn patterns, a few days to convert high-value prompts, and a few weeks to see organic standardization.
prompt.output_format: Return: 1) A 1-paragraph summary 2) Three bullets titled "Key takeaways" No headings beyond those labels.
